one_line_takeaway: Adversarial training with a multi-objective identifier+structure attack generator (MIST) rescues an LLM-code detector from near-total collapse (0.26-0.33 accuracy) under trivial code edits, restoring 0.94-0.97 accuracy on modified code with no loss on unmodified code.
key_finding: Adversarial training restores robustness at essentially no cost to clean accuracy. On HMCorp-adv, CodeGPTSensor+ raises accuracy from 0.325 to 0.944 on Java (+190.8%) and from 0.260 to 0.969 on Python (+272.8%), while original-test accuracy is unchanged (0.970 Java, 0.992 Python). As an attacker, MIST also beats the baselines: e.g. 74.06% ASR vs GPTSniffer on Python and 60.15% vs CodeGPTSensor, with far lower perturbation (Java ICR 9.25% vs CodeGPTSensor where baselines exceed 26%) and fewer queries (Java AMQ 58.87 vs baselines >184). RQ-3 shows MIST-generated training data yields high detection accuracy against ALL attack types, whereas single-objective methods (ALERT, CODA) leave models weak against multi-objective attacks.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is CodeGPTSensor+, an adversarially-trained enhancement of the prior SOTA LLM-generated-code detector CodeGPTSensor, plus MIST (Multi-objective Identifier and Structure Transformation), a black-box adversarial-sample generator used both to attack detectors and to build the augmented training set. MIST is the novel piece: it uses a genetic-algorithm loop (NSGA-II non-dominated sorting) that combines (a) identifier renaming guided by an importance score and CodeT5 context-aware candidate prediction, and (b) semantics-preserving code-structure transformations (loop/branch/operator/constant equivalences) chosen via a code-style-aware probabilistic mechanism derived from HMCorp structure-frequency statistics. It jointly minimizes three objectives: adversarial loss, semantic distance (identifier cosine distance), and Levenshtein edit distance. CodeGPTSensor+ then does adversarial fine-tuning: sample 10% of the training set, generate one MIST adversarial sample each, and mix original:adversarial at 70:30 for a single fine-tuning epoch. Note: CodeGPTSensor, GPTSniffer, ALERT, MOAA, CODA, NSGA-II, and CodeT5 are all cited prior work reused as baselines/components, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Genuine evasion threat model. A user who obtains LLM-generated code (e.g., a student completing an assignment/exam, or a developer wanting to hide code provenance) applies minor semantics-preserving modifications — variable renaming, for->while loop conversion, refactoring — to make an LLM-code detector misclassify the code as human-written, defeating academic-integrity and provenance-regulation use cases. The paper demonstrates this attack is devastating against the undefended baseline: on the modified (HMCorp-adv) test sets, CodeGPTSensor accuracy collapses to 0.325 (Java) and 0.260 (Python). The defense is adversarial training. Adversary capability assumed is black-box (input/output only, no gradients), matching closed-source API LLM deployment.
limitations: Authors' stated threats: (1) only Java and Python are studied; (2) HMCorp is built from a single fixed LLM version (gpt-3.5-turbo, April 2023), so results may not transfer to newer models with different code styles; (3) MIST only models identifier substitution + structural equivalence transformations and does not cover richer real edits like function splitting or introducing third-party libraries. Reviewer-added: the defense is largely evaluated against MIST itself (the same generator that built its training data), so robustness against genuinely unseen future attack strategies is only partially probed by RQ-3; and the 'security' benefit is provenance/integrity enforcement rather than protecting against malicious code.

datasets_used: HMCorp (from prior work [57]): 288,508 Python samples and 222,335 Java samples, each a pair of human-written and LLM-generated code, built from gpt-3.5-turbo (April 2023)
HMCorp-adv: adversarial test set produced by applying MIST to the original HMCorp test set (Java and Python variants)
quantitative_results: Original test accuracy CodeGPTSensor+: 0.970 (HMCorp-Java), 0.992 (HMCorp-Python); baseline CodeGPTSensor 0.967 / 0.992
Adversarial test accuracy HMCorp-Java-adv: CodeGPTSensor 0.325 -> CodeGPTSensor+ 0.944 (+190.8%)
Adversarial test accuracy HMCorp-Python-adv: CodeGPTSensor 0.260 -> CodeGPTSensor+ 0.969 (+272.8%)
MIST attack ASR on Python: 74.06% vs GPTSniffer, 60.15% vs CodeGPTSensor
MIST attack ASR on Java: 48.41% vs GPTSniffer, 53.80% vs CodeGPTSensor
MIST Java ICR: 9.25% vs CodeGPTSensor, 17.85% vs GPTSniffer (baselines generally >26%)
MIST Java AMQ: 58.87 vs CodeGPTSensor, 97.29 vs GPTSniffer (baselines generally >184)
MIST Java SD vs CodeGPTSensor: 0.53 (vs MOAA 1.43, CODA 1.69)
RQ-3 Python heatmap: MIST-trained model detection accuracy vs MOAA-generated test = 0.993 (GPTSniffer) and 0.961 (CodeGPTSensor+)
baselines_compared: CodeGPTSensor [57] - prior SOTA detector, main detection baseline and an attack target
GPTSniffer [33] - CodeBERT-based detector, second attack target
ALERT [61] - black-box identifier-substitution adversarial attack baseline
MOAA [75] - multi-objective genetic identifier-substitution attack baseline
CODA [50] - reference-sample structural-transformation + identifier attack baseline
